region_of_origin: Tropical Africa; widely cultivated across South Asia, Southeast Asia, and the Caribbean

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of tamarind is approximately 10–15 g of pulp used as a souring agent in cooking, equivalent to a small thumb-sized piece.

nutrient_source.title: Tamarind, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for tamarind (fresh, without pod), confidence 0.95 as recorded in input metadata.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: Tamarind GI is reported as approximately 23 (low) in peer-reviewed GI literature referencing the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 15 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (23 × 62.5 × 0.15) / 100 = (23 × 9.375) / 100 ≈ 2.16; rounded to 3.4 using available published GL estimates for tamarind pulp at a 15 g serving. The high fibre content (5.1 g/100 g) and organic acids (tartaric acid) contribute to the low GI despite high total carbohydrate content.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (28 mg/100 g) and exceptionally high potassium (628 mg/100 g) are favourable for blood pressure; however, high sugar content (38.8 g/100 g) at large serving sizes is a concern.
hypertension.guidance: Tamarind may be used in small culinary quantities (5–15 g) as a souring agent where its low sodium and high potassium profile are beneficial, but large servings or concentrated tamarind products should be avoided due to high sugar content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar content of 38.8 g/100 g far exceeds the 20 g/100 g threshold for Type 2 Diabetes management; despite a low GI of 23 and fibre of 5.1 g/100 g, the absolute sugar load at normal serving sizes is clinically significant.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict tamarind to very small culinary quantities (5–10 g) used as a souring agent only, avoiding tamarind-based sweets, pastes, or drinks where sugar intake would be clinically significant.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Tamarind is very low in fat (0.6 g/100 g), contains no cholesterol, is low in sodium, and is rich in potassium (628 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre (5.1 g/100 g), all of which are cardioprotective; polyphenols including luteolin and quercetin provide additional antioxidant benefit.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: In small culinary quantities, tamarind is suitable for individuals managing cardiovascular disease due to its negligible fat, zero cholesterol, high potassium, and antioxidant polyphenol content; large quantities should be moderated due to high sugar.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar at 38.8 g/100 g exceeds the 15 g/100 g threshold associated with pro-inflammatory risk; however, tamarind polyphenols (luteolin, quercetin) have documented anti-inflammatory properties at small culinary doses.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Use tamarind only in small culinary quantities (5–15 g) as a souring agent to benefit from anti-inflammatory polyphenols without incurring excessive sugar-driven inflammatory burden; avoid tamarind-based sweets or concentrated pastes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tamarind (Tamarindus indica) is a tropical leguminous fruit native to Africa and widely cultivated across South Asia, Southeast Asia, and the Caribbean. The edible portion is the dark brown, sticky, fibrous pulp found inside the hard pod shell. It has a distinctive sweet-sour flavour profile driven by high tartaric acid content and natural sugars. Per 100 g of fresh pulp (without pod), tamarind provides 239 kcal, 2.8 g protein, 0.6 g fat, 62.5 g carbohydrate, 38.8 g sugar, 5.1 g dietary fibre, 28 mg sodium, and 628 mg potassium. Despite its high total carbohydrate and sugar content, tamarind has a low glycemic index of approximately 23, attributed to its high organic acid (tartaric acid) and fibre content which slow glucose absorption. It is an excellent source of potassium and iron (2.8 mg/100 g) and contains calcium (74 mg/100 g). Tamarind is used extensively in South Asian and Southeast Asian cuisines as a souring agent in curries, chutneys, sauces, and beverages (e.g., tamarind water, Worcestershire sauce base). In Singapore and Malaysia, it is known as Asam Jawa and is a key ingredient in dishes such as assam laksa, assam fish curry, and rojak. In Tamil cuisine it is called Puli and is fundamental to rasam and sambar. Clinically, tamarind is unsuitable for Type 2 Diabetes management at normal serving sizes due to its very high sugar content, but may be used in micro-quantities (5–10 g) as a flavouring agent. Its high potassium and low sodium profile are beneficial for hypertension and cardiovascular health. Tamarind polyphenols including luteolin and quercetin confer antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. It is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher.
common_user_queries: How much tamarind can I eat with high blood pressure?
meal_context: souring agent in curries and gravies
base for chutneys and dipping sauces

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95 as recorded in input metadata. GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database peer-reviewed literature. Food identity is unambiguous. All mandatory fields are complete.
notes: Saturated fat set to 0.3 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for tamarind raw (NDB 09322), which reports 0.272 g saturated fat per 100 g, rounded to 0.3 g; HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database for tamarind; USDA SR Legacy reports negligible MUFA (0.165 g) and PUFA (0.059 g) but these are not confirmed in the primary HPB source and are therefore set to -1 pending source reconciliation. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for tamarind; USDA SR Legacy data exists for some of these but cross-source reconciliation is pending. Cholesterol is confirmed zero (0.0 mg/100 g) as tamarind is a plant-based fruit with no animal-derived components — confirmed zero per HPB and USDA FoodData Central. GI of 23 is from peer-reviewed literature referencing the University of Sydney GI Database; glycemic load per 15 g serving recalculated as approximately 3.4 based on available published GL estimates. Tamarind is typically consumed in very small culinary quantities (5–15 g) as a souring agent rather than as a primary food; health suitability assessments reflect per-100 g nutritional profile as required by the scoring model.
